1
The 1-2-3 Formula: 2 Stories
The Nightmare Story
ALL CONTENT © COPYRIGHT 2011 AND BEYOND | JON BENSON 3X
FORMULAS, INC. | ALL RIGHTS RESERVED | NO DUPLICATION ALLOWED
In this session, we’re going to cover the 1-2-3 Formula. These are the 4
elements that I have discovered that exist in virtually every top-performing video
sales letter that I’ve analyzed over the past year.
We’ve already covered #1: that is the USP. #2 is there’s 2 stories: there’s 2
stories, and sometimes more, in every successful VSL.
You may be thinking to yourself, “I can’t write a good story. I’m not a good
writer.” You’re going to discover that you’re much better at it than you think; you
just need the formula that I’m about to give you.
There are 2 forms of stories that we want to discuss today. One of them is called
“the nightmare story.”
Now, the nightmare story is your personal story of hitting bottom, before you
discovered blank. Now, 9 times out of 10, this is going to work for most people’s
businesses. If it’s a paper-shredding company, maybe there isn’t a nightmare
story. Maybe the nightmare story deals with what the client went through. You
tell a horrible story of what the client went through.
But most of the time, maybe you had some documents stolen and that’s what got
you into this business, that’s really compelling.
When people hear this, they are immediately drawn in. And I’m going to cover
the psychology of why here in a few minutes.
But this is your personal story of hitting rock-bottom, before you discovered
blank. And that blank is whatever the product is that you’re selling.

-- 1 of 17 --

2
The product that you’re selling is a solution to a problem. Notice that we started
with what is the solution to the problem. What problem does this solve? That is
what you’re selling. You may think that you’re selling a service, you may think
you’re selling a book. You are selling a solution. And that word is critical to
understand.
Now, again, you may think you don’t have one, but this system is going to pull it
out of you. Trust me.
The second story is what I call “the dream story.” What is that? That is a vivid
picture of what your prospect’s life is going to look like after they purchase your
product.
This is called “future pacing,” and it’s one of the most vital sales elements that
you’ll ever, ever use.
Let’s cover the nightmare story first. Get the Word document out, get the text
editor out, whatever you’re using to type on. If you’re using it online, great. Just
let’s get going.
The nightmare story.
This should not be fiction, even if you’re doing this as a fictional character.
A good friend of mine, Eben Pagan, wrote as David DeAngelo for a long time.
David DeAngelo’s story is somewhat Eben Pagan’s story. So, it should not be
fiction. You want this to be a true story, because you want it to come out as
authentic as possible.
However, the events of that story can be out of sequence. It’s kind of like when
you see a movie, and it’s a biopic movie, where the movie is telling some story
that happened in the past, like the life of Babe Ruth. They put all the facts
together, but then they kind of make a difference sequence for it because it
makes for better entertainment.
Well, this is no different. And the sole purpose behind this is to enter into a state
of rapport with your prospect. You want to enter a conversation that’s already
going on within your prospect’s mind. That is the definition of ultimate marketing:
to enter a conversation that’s already going on within your prospect’s mind. And
one way you can do that is to tell your own nightmare story.
Now, when someone hits your sales page or your video sales letter, whatever it
may be, they are in one or more of these 3 states. There’s 3 states of mind.
There’s what I call “the disbelief mode.” Everybody has a little bit of doubt when
they hit a sales page. Everybody. If they’re seeing something, they’re going to
have a little bit of doubt. And the way around disbelief is to build rapport. That’s
why we’re doing this.

-- 2 of 17 --

3
Now, your prospect does believe one thing, and this may shock you. You are
better than they are. They believe this. You are better than they are. You have
a website; they probably don’t. You have a solution to a problem; they don’t.
You appear to be famous, even if you’re not; they probably are not.
So, this is actually not good for selling. It’s not good for selling. And this is one
of the biggest mistakes that I see in sales copy. You won’t believe how powerful
what I’m about to cover is, if you just tweak it a little bit.
Your job is to convince your readers that you are only slightly better off than they
are. Slightly more advanced. And the reason why is because you will never
enter into a state of rapport with someone who believes that you are too far
above them.
This is what I call “the expert’s paradox,” because at some point you’re going to
make a case for you being an expert in your field, and you want to do that.
But before you do that, you tell a story. And once you tell a story people can
relate to, like, “Wow, this guy or this girl, she was kind of where I was, and she
really had to rise above this.” This is called “the expert’s paradox.”
You want to set yourself up as being an expert later down the line. And first, you
want to set yourself up as someone that went through a really horrendous
nightmare story that relates to what your user probably feels. That is key.
We’re going to call this “the expert from necessity.” That is the secret to all
nightmare stories. You became an expert from necessity.
Let me give you an example. I will show you this in print, on-screen here, in a
little bit. But let me give you just a quick example before we move on. And I’ll
use this for my own life.
I used to be obese. So, I became a fitness model and a fitness guru, and blah,
blah, blah, because of necessity. I was literally dying.
So, I tell a very vivid story. I tell several. I actually had several nightmare stories.
So, I tell different ones.
The one that you’re going to hear today is actually true. The only thing that’s not
true is it wasn’t donuts. Just write that down: it was not donuts, it was something
else. But it’s close enough to donuts, but people wouldn’t understand the
reference unless they were local.
So, anyway, my nightmare story is so freaking embarrassing, and I’m going to
share it.

-- 3 of 17 --

4
I became an expert on weight loss out of necessity. What you want to have your
prospect understand is that you became an expert in this field because you had
to. Your back was against the wall. You were at a very dark point in your life.
How many people believe that when people hit a website, that everybody’s in a
great mood and they’re joyous about their mood? No one believes that.
Most people who are looking for a solution-oriented problem are in some sort of
pain and some sort of doubt. And this is the doubt part, the disbelief mode.
When you praise yourself as an expert from necessity, you will disarm the
disbelief.
The next is what I call “the decision mode.”
Now, all website visitors are in decision mode. Now, all website visitors are in
decision mode. You might think that that might be a good thing. No, it’s not a
good thing. It’s not a good thing.
Your goal is to move your viewers from a state of decision to a state of desire.
Okay? That’s a very key distinction. A state of decision to a state of desire.
When you desire something, you’re like, “How much is it? I don’t really care.
Just give it to me. I want it right now.”
When you’re in a state of decision, you’re thinking things like, “I wonder if I can
afford this? I wonder if this is the best product? I wonder if there’s a better deal
out there? I wonder if my wife or my boyfriend would get upset?”
You’ve got to take anyone watching your VSL out of that state, and put them into
a state of absolute desire. And this is done during these 2 stories, as well as the
grand offer. The grand offer is our last session. So, hang with us.
The grand offer is absolutely one of the key, key places to instill this desire.
Then, there’s discovery mode. All website visitors want to discover something.
That’s probably why they’re hitting your site. But the first minute of your video
sales letter must convey a feeling of future discovery. You’re going to let the
viewer know that they’re going to discover something. You’ll want to write this
down: “If they watch until the end.” If they watch until the end.
Now, that phrase is popular now, on almost every VSL because, frankly, I started
it. So, “watch until the very end” was something that we started at the very
beginning.
When I show you the psychology behind this, you may go, “Ooh, that’s a little too
ninja. That’s sinister.” But that’s okay, because if your goal is to actually sell a
product that helps people, I believe in doing it in any honest way possible, in any

-- 4 of 17 --

5
ethical way possible. All of these tricks are ethical and honest. I do not believe
in dishonest marketing, ever. Ever! Just so we get that out of the way.
People love to feel as if they’ve discovered something new. I’ll bet you do, too.
Something exciting, something that answers a pressing question, something
that’s been keeping them up at night, they find an answer to it.
If you start off by saying, “Hey, you’re going to find an answer to this,” it’s going
to keep them glued to the screen.
We’re going to be using terms like this, like “exciting,” “discover,” “new”
throughout your VSL.
Okay, here’s the importance of anchoring your most powerful emotional states to
those of your reader. This happens, by the way, during both the nightmare story
and the dream story. You’re anchoring your most powerful emotional states to
those of your reader. In other words, you want to bond, you want to build a
rapport with your reader. And you do that on an emotional level. Even if you’re
selling a logical product, people don’t buy out of logical reasons, usually. There’s
only about 15% of the buyers that buy logically. Most of the buyers buy
emotionally.
We’re going to cover all 3 states, by the way, later on.
So, the importance of anchoring. Here we go.
Here’s the formula for the nightmare story. All of this, by the way, anchors the
emotional states. This is the whole idea behind it.
The very first part of the formula is the transition. And it goes something like,
here’s an example, “I wasn’t always the person you see today. I wasn’t always
an expert in fitness. I wasn’t always…” and you just drop a subtle hint that you
are pretty much a bad-ass, that you know what you’re doing.
Our company wasn’t always the best paper-shredding company in the world. I
wasn’t always the best private detective in the world, whatever you may be. I
wasn’t always a top-level fill-in-the-blank. That is the transition into a story you’re
about to tell.
Then, we start with the intro. Don’t worry, I’m going to cover every single one of
these in a lot of detail on the following pages, but we’re giving you the overview
of the formula.
The intro is “It was a dark and stormy night.” Please don’t write “It was a dark
and stormy night,” even if it was. Everybody knows that as being a really hokey
introduction to a bad novel. But the intro is kind of like that. And you’ll see what I
mean. We’re going to give you the exact step formula to write that.

-- 5 of 17 --

6
Then, you’re going to get into what I call “the bad part.” This is some bad stuff
that’s going on, kind of a negative zone in your life, negative event that’s going
on. But it all leads to the really bad part, and that is the focal point: one event in
your life that had you hit rock bottom. I’ll give you some examples here in a bit.
Then, a ray of hope. It’s called the declaration of independence. You make a
declaration at this point, to never do blank again, that I will figure out blank, no
matter what. I’ll give you specifics.
Finally, there’s the moral, and that is your personal journey to freedom or
whatever it may be that you are offering. If you’re offering a service, it’s your
journey to figure out that this service is so necessary. If it’s like me, and I’m
offering a diet plan to people, my journey to being lean, my freedom from my
obesity, those kind of things, that is the moral of the story.
The whole idea behind the nightmare story is to take someone down into the
deepest part of the most negative experiences of your life, that caused you to
become an expert, by necessity. In other words, you’re actually a little worse
than they are, and then you come up to being an expert. And once you get that
dip, “I’m a little worse than you have ever been, I bet you money,” and “Yeah,
now I’m kind of an expert,” they’re going to feel like, “This person’s a little better
than me, but they’re not so far above me that I can’t do it.” Does that make
sense?
Let’s get into the specifics.
The intro. This begins the story of when you hit rock bottom. And it happens
before discovering what you’re going to share with your reader, whatever your
solution may be. That’s how this starts off. And here’s how you create it.
You’re going to create this using what I call “the specifics strategy.” Here, I want
you to start taking a lot of notes. I’m going to go slow, to make sure that you get
it all.
You’re going to ask yourself, “When was it?” When was your personal event? I
want to know the date, down to the day and time, if possible, literally. Specifics
create fantastic copy. And anyone can be specific.
This is one of a copywriter’s most secret, cherished tricks of the trade. They hold
this back. But if you look at any great story, it’s always really specific. If you
read a great novel, it covers every little detail. That’s what makes it great.
Well, you can do this, and here’s how you start. When was it? The date, down
to the time. What was the weather like?
Now, people might think, “Who cares what the weather was like?” I’m going to
show you a couple examples of why you should care.

-- 6 of 17 --

7
Let’s say it was raining. “I was driving, and I was scared that I might crash.” So,
you can build a little bit of tension.
“It was a sunny, beautiful day.” And if you’re selling a weight loss product,
“Everybody else was probably at the beach, but I hated my body so much I
couldn’t enjoy the beautiful, sunny day. I remember that.”
So, why did you remember it was a beautiful, sunny day? Because you
remembered you couldn’t go to the beach.”
“It was cloudy and gloomy, and maybe that weather reflected my depression over
whatever it is, the bad part, the focal point, this depression that you’re about to
enter into.”
That’s why the weather’s important. Plus, when you mention the weather, the
story becomes something people can touch and feel.
You say, “I remember it was Tuesday, sometime in November, and I remember
that it was really, really cloudy that day.” You’re telling a story that is really
pulling people into the narrative.
What were you wearing? This is some great examples: 2 jackets. “I was
wearing 2 jackets, because I was trying to hide my gut.” I had one tight jacket on
underneath, and then the big jacket on top.” I actually did that once, by the way.
“I was wearing clothes from Kmart. Not because I was a smart shopper, but
because I was a broke shopper.”
You’re going to hear me talk about this throughout entire course. You want to let
the reader off the hook just long enough to make them realize that they need to
be on the hook. And you’re going to offer them a hook, along with the bait, along
with the prize. That is your grand offer. But they have to be let off the hook a
little bit, because they feel pretty crappy right now.
For example, if someone has to shop at Kmart and they don’t have the money to
shop somewhere else, you compliment them for being a smart shopper. But in
your case, “it wasn’t because I was a smart,” because it is kind of smart to shop
at Kmart some of the time, “but it was because I was broke.” You see how that
paints a vivid picture?
I’m going to go back through this again.
When was it? What was the weather like? And what were you wearing? This is
just the start.
Next, who were you with? If it’s a weight loss product, “My skinny friend, Lisa,” if
she was skinny. That’s great. That actually is from a real story, not from me but
from something I wrote.

-- 7 of 17 --

8
“Alone, as usual.” If you have a dating product, a relationship-building product,
that’s powerful. “Alone, as usual.”
Or, if you have a financial product, you could say, “I was with my financial
advisor, James.” Whoever you were with. It doesn’t even matter if it’s important
to the story. In my personal story, it has no bearing on the story at all. But I use
Steve because I was with Steve. And when I tell my audience that I was with my
friend Steve, it makes my story that much more believable, first of all, because
that is the truth. And second of all, it makes it that much more real.
The goal here is to pull your reader into a state of dismissing – remember, we’re
trying to dismiss that decision mode – and into literally absorbing them into a
story. And this is how you do it. The more real, the more factual, the more little
details and strategic details that you put in, the better.
Next is where were you? I was at the downtown office, and we have a joke in
here, “I was in Greenville, Alabama.” Forrest Gump joke, on the side of the road
on I-10. Get very specific. Where was I? What city? What road? If it matters.
You can get as specific as you want here. Where were you?
And what were you feeling up until this moment, the moment right before the big
focal point happens? “I was excited because I just had a birthday,” as an
example. So, you were feeling excited.
What if you were scared? “I was scared of being caught daydreaming again by
my boss.” So, this is what you were feeling before something really tragic
happened.
In my case, I was feeling anxious and I was feeling hungry. That’s my personal
story. So, it can be anything you were feeling at the moment.
Now, I’m going to use the word “feeling” a lot, because why? Because you bond
through feeling. You do not bond through logic. We’re going to write logically,
because you want to attract a logical buyer. I’ll show you how in another
session. But you’re going to write primarily emotionally, especially during the
nightmare story and the dream story, because that’s what people relate to the
strongest.
Next up is the bad part. This starts the downward spiral. And it begins by
connecting what’s called a related state.
What I mean by related state is you’re going to connect your reader emotionally,
to build stronger rapport, just like we were just talking about. However, you’re
going to do so by saying something positive. Just for a moment, you’re going to
take them out of that negative dark gloom and say something positive, or
something that they want. That’s called a related state. You both want
something.

-- 8 of 17 --

9
So, in my case, I wanted to lose weight, and I mentioned that to my reader. “I
know you want this, too. I know you want this too.” So, this is a related state,
right before you set up the bad part.
I’m going to give you an example.
“At this point in my life, I thought that things could not get worse, although they
were about to. And I’ll share that with you in a moment.” This is relational part.
“Perhaps you can relate to feeling this, and know that you need to change too.”
See, knowing you need to change is not necessarily negative. That’s actually
positive. The change can be positive. You can get more specific here.
“Knowing that you want to have the body of your dreams. Maybe you feel that,
too.” Or, “Having the financial independence you’ve always longed for.” That is
called relationship states.
So, you want to pull people back up into a positive zone, just for a moment,
before you go into this very negative part.
Notice how I’m about to lay a whopper of a story on somebody. “At this point in
my life, it couldn’t get worse, although it was about to. Things were about to get
a lot worse. I’m going to share it with you in a moment.” And then, you pull them
briefly out, tell them something positive that your product does, and you continue.
Go right back at it.
This is what I call the “one more thing transition.” It’s a phrase. “I knew I was in
a position where I needed to change, but one more thing had to happen before I
really got it.”
Now, that’s just an example of how you would write it. That’s sort of covered in
the example above. “Things were about to get worse. They were about to get a
lot worse.” But this one horrible event had to happen before they got worse,
before I finally woke up. And the one event is leading up to what? The focal
point. The really, really bad part.
So, the one thing that had to happen. You want to make sure that your focal
point, when you get down to the really bad part, that it’s one event, it’s one thing
that happens. Make it one thing.
There may be 10 things, but make it one. Pick one.
Okay, here’s the focal point, the really bad part. Here’s your intro.
The event right before the focal point is what is called the epicenter of your rock
bottom story, by the way. That’s the focal point. This is what happened right
before this really bad thing happened.

-- 9 of 17 --

10
So, right before I did something really horrible, that I feel really, really bad about,
right before that, something happened. It can be something as simple as I just
walked through a door, I walked into my mother’s room. It could be anything.
You just need an intro into your focal point.
So, here’s an example, and this is from mine.
“Steve and I pulled into this convenient store.” Okay, that sets up the location. I
want you to notice that, that the intro to the focal point is location-based almost
all the time.
So, the bad thing that happened to you, the really bad part, happened at a
location somewhere. You’re going to let your reader know what that location
was, and how you got to that location.
So, Steve and I pulled into a convenience store. The convenience store is the
site of my focal point. That’s when all the bad stuff happened. Okay?
Another example would be, “I drove around by myself until I came across this
empty house.” Well, the empty house is obviously where everything happened.
So, wherever everything happened is your intro to the focal point, and it can be
as simple as saying, “I drove myself around” or “I walked into the door.” Let’s say
it’s a financial product. “I walked into the bank, until I realized I had overdrawn
my account by $10,000,” or something horrible like that.
So, that’s all there is to it. But in these examples, again, the focal point always
occurs within the convenient store or an empty house. That’s the goal. Where
does it happen? Where does it happen within?
Then, the transition is simple. “And then, it happened…” This is the really
powerful narrative. I’m going to give you 3 phrases that work every single time.
“And then, it happened…” “And then, I did it…” And people are going, “You did
what?”
This is going to be a on slide all by itself, by the way, when we put the VSL
together.
And then I did what? You’ve got everybody on pins and needles, at this point.
One of my favorites, “And then, my worst fears came true.” Again, this is the
transition to the actual event itself. You walked into this convenience store, “And
then, I did it. I can’t believe it, but I actually did it. My worst fears came true.”
You could actually use 2. That creates a very compelling story, right to the focal
point. Okay?
Now, you’re going to want to describe the focal point. This is a shameful act or
moment in your life. I’m asking you guys to get a little personal here. That’s

-- 10 of 17 --

11
great. It’s okay. You can do that. And you want to do that. You want to convey
this type of vulnerability and honesty to your reader. It tells not only a great story,
but it builds rapport.
So, usually, this is a shameful act, and it should sting a little bit when you tell it.
I’m going to give you an entire example of mine, and you’ll get that in a PDF as
well, that tells one of my stories. I have several nightmare stories. And it should
hurt. It should feel like, “Ah, I don’t feel like sharing this.” It’s like sharing my
before pictures with people. I hate doing that.
That’s okay. That’s what you want, because this is the one thing that made you
completely bottom out. This was the final straw. This was it. This was the
turning point in your life.
Now, we’re going to bring everybody out, including you.
How did you feel after the focal point? This is the beginning of coming out,
although it’s going to sound really negative. “Lost. I felt really lost. I felt
confused. I felt disappointed in myself. I felt angry.” Something that you felt.
What did you feel immediately after the focal point happened? So, we’re starting
to lift out of this now, because you’re about to declare independence from
whatever it is that you were stuck in. Whatever it is the focal point was, you’re
going to declare independence from that lifestyle. And this is what made you the
expert.
Here are some examples.
“I made a vow.”
“I decided, at that moment, to make a change in my life.”
“I made the most important decision I’ve ever made.”
Those are just some brief examples of declarations. Declarations are pretty
easy. At some point, when something really bad happened to you, or you went
through a really tough time. And I’m going to make up an analogy here.
Let’s say that you have a relationship-building product, and you went through a
divorce. And that’s what made you hurt so much, that you decided to create a
product on relationships. Maybe it was your second divorce.
You made a vow, at some point, after being divorced 2 times, and going through
this horrible, horrible loss, where you felt like crying all the time, “I made a vow,
never again. Never again. I decided to do…” what? Blank. And that’s what
comes next.
What was your vow, and what was your decision?

-- 11 of 17 --

12
“I will never, ever be this way again.” This is from a person’s literal site on
attracting other people, usually women. “I will get this attraction thing handled or
I will die trying.” That’s pretty bold. That’s a declaration of independence. “I am
going to get this attraction to women down, or I’m going to die trying.”
For me, it was, “I will never let food imprison me again. Never.”
That is a declaration. You can write this however you’d like, but it’s pretty simple
once you think about what will you never do again. “No matter what, this is going
to happen.”
I continue on, in one of my stories. I said, “I vowed to read every book in the
library on nutrition.” I vowed to read every book in my personal library, not in the
library, on nutrition. It ended up being like 300 books. It was ridiculous.
So, what was your personal decision? What made you become the expert – the
reluctant expert, if you will? I didn’t want to be an expert in weight loss. I didn’t
start off that way.”
You see how this makes me relate so well to someone who might be going
through a weight loss issue? Well, it works in any industry. In any industry, this
works. It doesn’t matter what it is, you can make this work.
Next up is the moral. This is your personal journey to freedom. What happened
after your declaration? This is your dedication declaration. This is what you
decide to do. Remember what I said, “Reading everything there is to know about
blank.” In my case, nutrition. I decided to read everything there is to know,
everything I could get my hands on, about how best to make money, how best to
live the Internet lifestyle, how best to have a relationship that lasts.
Another one is “I decided to get a college degree, no matter what it took, and not
even pay for it.” So, you can make a bold claim here.
This is a real one. “I decided to be the first in my family to move out of the trailer
park.” That’s pretty strong. Obviously, if that’s not true, you don’t want to use it.
But what did you decide? The moment after you felt that focal point pain, you
made a decision, you made a vow, you made a promise. What was it? That
promise is the absolute key to transitioning your reader into why you are the
expert and why they absolutely have to listen to the solution you have for them
today.
So, the result of that is what? These are the power phrases, people.
“I uncovered a secret.” “I discovered a pattern.” “I formulated a blueprint.” And
this always ties back to what? Your product or your service.

-- 12 of 17 --

13
You can go on here. “I discovered the very best way to blank.” “I discovered a
hidden pathway to blank. These are the phrases that really work: “hidden,”
“secret,” “pattern,” those kind of things. “Blueprint,” “formula.” I use “formula.”
You might notice that. “Formula,” that works. “I developed a formula.”
Now, in my case, with my own nightmare story with this particular product that
you’re watching right now, my particular formula was that I just didn’t know why
what I had created worked. I actually told this story when I spoke at Dan
Kennedy not too long ago. I didn’t know why. I didn’t know why this ugly video
sales letter thing worked. I just knew that I had to figure it out. And after
dissecting hundreds of them, I uncovered a pattern. It’s basically a formula. So,
I use 2.
“There was a pattern, and it is a formula.” It ties right back to my product or my
service. That’s the result of the nightmare story formula.
You have one final transition. It’s very simple. And you guys can all use this one
specifically. Use this specifically: “And today, I’m going to share it with you.”
That’s what people want to hear. They want to hear that you have uncovered a
secret, “A secret I’m going to share with you.” And you want to include one more
phrase, “if you watch until the end of this video.” We’re going to include that
phrase frequently in your VSL. “Keep watching, and I’ll share the secret with you
today.” “Keep watching, and I’ll share my blueprint with you today. I’ll share
what I discovered, that pattern that I discovered with you today. Just keep
watching.”
You’re not ready to go into it yet. This is setting people up for copy.
What’s going to go next? That’s in another session. But your pitch does not go
here. I’ve seen people put their pitch here, what we call “the grand offer.” It’s
one of the worst places to put it. One of the worst places to put it. Because right
now, you have just bonded with this person on a deep, emotional level. And
you’ve connected to some of their greatest fears. You’ve connected to some of
their greatest fears. And you’ve shown them how you are just an average
person, maybe a little better, you’ve gotten a little better by necessity, how you
went through it, how you came out of it, and what you discovered. “I discovered
the light.” “I discovered the secret, the blueprint, and I’m going to share it with
you today. So, keep watching.” Keep watching – 2 simple words.
That is the nightmare story formula. It’s pretty simple. And if you want, we can
go back over it.
I will give you my example nightmare story first.
So, guys, girls, whoever may be listening to this, get out your handkerchiefs, get
out your tissues. I will go ahead and read this. It’s probably small on the screen.

-- 13 of 17 --

14
I remember the day when I decided enough was enough. I’m
going to share the story with you now, even though it’s really
embarrassing. I think you need to know how deep down the rabbit
hole I went before I discovered the stuff I’m going to share with you
today, that now allows me to live this amazing life.
I remember it was late on a Tuesday night, and my friend Steve
and I were leaving the office. It was really cold outside. And the
reason I remember that is going to be really clear in just a moment.
Notice how I’m actually putting an anchor inside the story itself? I’m asking you
to keep reading.
“I’m going to tell you why, in just a moment.”’ Those are called “positioning
anchors,” and you want to use them.
I walked outside, and I got halfway to my car, and I realized I’d left
my coat in my office. It was cold enough to where I realized it was
cold, but it just crossed my mind, and I barely feel it. Is this
because I’m so fat? Is that why I’m not feeling cold? I think it was.
I was insulated by all this ugly blubber.
I was thinking, “Oh my god!” It had already been a really rough
day, and now I’m like, “Wow, I already feel tired, and it’s not even
7:00 pm.” I felt really crappy, as I always did. But this day was
really bad. Knowing that I had my own insulation just made me
feel like crap. So, I didn’t want to go get my coat, at this point. I
just got in the car and said, “Steve, drive me to the nearest
convenience store. I want some donuts.”
Can you see how I’m going right towards the focal point now? It all happened at
a convenience store. “Steve, drive me to a convenience store, I want some
donuts.”
That’s how I was going to medicate myself: wolf down some
donuts.
We were driving to a convenience store, and it’s raining. So, the
car is sliding all the way. And I’m thinking to myself, “Maybe it’s
because I’m so heavy, it’s weighted on one side.” Now, I know that
probably wasn’t true, but this is how I was feeling.
Again, I want you to notice how I emphasize the word “feeling.” How was I
feeling at this moment? Remember, we went through these steps with you. How
were you feeling when this was going on? You can reflect how you were feeling
throughout the entire nightmare story process. It’s fine.

-- 14 of 17 --

15
We drove past a corner, not too far from a convenience store, and I
noticed 2 prostitutes standing on the corner. I could tell by looking
at them that they were probably – and sadly – addicted to crack.
At this point, you’re probably going, “Where is he going with this? What does this
have to do with weight loss?” Right? Well, I’m building tension.
I’m not being funny when I say this.
I thought to myself, “I’m addicted to a different kind of crack. I’m
addicted to sugar. Sugar is like my crack, and I’m addicted. There
is no difference.”
What I’m doing here is I’m building external rapport. This is a little bit of an
advanced technique, but I’m building rapport with something else that’s so far
below what most people would look at you and I or anyone being, a crack whore
on the sidewalk. That’s a pretty low level of existence, not being insulting, but
definitely something we don’t want to live. And I’m saying, “I can relate to these
people. I’m addicted, too. My addiction just doesn’t happen to be crack, it’s
sugar. Is there really a difference?”
And I go on:
“To top it all off, I’d sold myself out, too.” That’s what I started
thinking…
And I put in the word “thinking” here, just to pull in anyone that’s isn’t too feely-
touchy.
…thinking I sold myself out. I’ve become a food whore. Seriously.
This is how bad my addiction to food and self-hatred had become.
I’m painting a pretty dark picture.
But things were about to get a lot worse.
And now you’re probably going, “Okay, what on earth?” That’s what I want you
to think.
I walked into the convenience store, grabbed my 2 boxes of
donuts, roughly a night snack at that time in my life, and proceeded
to the checkout counter, when I noticed that I’d left my wallet in the
office, in my coat.
See how the coat ties back to this whole story? I didn’t take my coat. I had left
my wallet in the coat.

-- 15 of 17 --

16
In a split second, I decided to do something I never, ever thought
I’d do. I stole those donuts. I hid them in my jeans, and the clerk
didn’t even look twice at me, because my jeans were so big and
baggy in the legs, yet tight in the waist, so it was easy to slip those
boxes right into my underwear and hold them against my legs as I
walked out of the store.
In total shame and with a feeling of absolute desperation, I wolfed
down half a box during the ride home.
This is how I felt after the focal point. The focal point is rather obvious: I stole
donuts. They weren’t really donuts, but we’re using donuts here for this story.
But this really happened, by the way.
So, I stole the sugary snack. This is actually how I felt. I felt totally shameful. I’d
never stolen anything in my life. Maybe a pencil from school or something. And I
was feeling completely desperate, at this point. How bad is this going to get?
The depression was getting really, really bad.
So, I say:
I guarantee you, when I tell you I hit rock bottom, my rock bottom
was pretty freaking low. Pretty freaking low.
So, right now, I am getting complete rapport. In fact, most users are probably
feeling sorry for me. They came onto the website feeling sorry for themselves.
They’re probably going, Man, that guy really had it bad!”
You see how I took myself off the expert pedestal and how I’m building the
rapport? But now, everything turns around.
But you know what? That one day caused my entire life to change.
I vowed to read every book and crawl under every rock, if
necessary, to break my addiction to sugar and to lose my excess
weight or die trying.
The good news for you is this: not only did I figure out how to drop
the weight and keep it off, I figured out how to enjoy my donuts
while doing it.
Notice how I tied the actual focal point back into something positive?
I actually figured out how to do this and enjoy it, which is true, by
the way. I will share my discovery with you in just a moment, so
keep watching.
Okay, folks, that is an example of a nightmare story. And something we probably
need to discuss here is length.

-- 16 of 17 --

17
Don’t worry about length. People are concerned about how long does this go on.
Is 15 minutes too long in a video sales letter? No. The average video sales
letter that works really well is usually around 28 minutes to 32 minutes. Believe it
or not, that’s true.

-- 17 of 17 --
